steps.annotation: struct keyword introduces a NAMED bundle of related fields. Here the bundle is called 'book'.
First field: a string named title. One field per line.
Close brace AND semicolon end the struct definition.
Now book is a usable type. novel is one bundle of three fields.

levelLabel: L11 -> related fields under one name

steps.annotation: A struct gathers logically related fields under one name -- here 'book'.
Each field has its type and name. They all live inside one book together.
b is a book -- it has a title field AND a pages field, bundled together.
struct book is a TYPE. It is not yet a value -- it's a recipe for values.
one is the first instance of book.
two is a second, independent instance.
Each book has its own title field. one and two are separate.

teachMe: novel is one book with two fields. Print title then year separated by space.
Print just the pages field of book 'one'. Output: 412.
Two book instances are independent. Each has its own title field.
Multiple writes to b.year overwrite the field. Final value: 2024.

clozeSentence: Fill in the keyword that introduces a named field group
explanation: struct is the C++ keyword for declaring a named bundle of fields.

code: // A struct holds related ___ under one name.
clozeSentence: What kinds of things are bundled inside a struct?
explanation: A struct is a named bundle of fields (each with its own type and name).

clozeSentence: Declare an instance of book named 'novel'
explanation: After defining struct book, you can declare instances: 'book novel;'.
year is the second field. Each line is one field.

spec: Write the keyword that begins a struct definition
expectedAnswer: struct
explanation: struct is the keyword to introduce a named bundle of fields.

spec: Complete the struct definition: book with one string field title (don't forget };)
explanation: struct + name + { fields } + };  -- the basic shape of a struct definition.

spec: Write a struct named 'book' with three fields: string title, string author, int year. Don't forget the trailing };.
explanation: Three fields, each on its own line, ending with };.

spec: Write a struct named 'book' with two fields: string title, int pages. End with };.
explanation: Simple two-field struct. struct keyword, fields, };.

spec: Write a struct named 'book' with two fields: string title, double rating. End with };.
explanation: Field types are int, double, string, etc. Each field type then name then ;.

levelLabel: L11 -> struct Name { fields }; defines a TYPE (employee)

steps.annotation: After this line up to };, you are DEFINING a new type called employee_data.
Once };is reached, employee_data is now a usable type just like int or string.
Use it like any type: 'employee_data e;' creates an instance.

levelLabel: L11 -> definition makes a new type

steps.annotation: Defining the type. employee_data is a TEMPLATE/SHAPE, not a value.
After the type exists, you can instantiate as many as you want.
Each instance has its own copy of all fields, totally independent.
The closing brace must be followed by a semicolon. } alone = compile error.
After }; the type is registered. Code below can declare instances.
Output: '7 Bo'. Each field accessed via e.fieldname.

teachMe: Once struct defined with };, e is an employee_data instance. Output: '7 Bo'.
Two independent employee_data instances; each has its own name field.
Use struct fields like normal variables. 50000 + 1500 = 51500. Output: '42 51500'.
Reassigning a struct field overwrites it. Output: 'Lin 5'.

clozeSentence: Symbol(s) needed at the very end
explanation: After the closing } of a struct definition, you MUST write a semicolon.
After 'struct' comes the type name -- here employee_data.

code:
// After 'struct employee_data { ... };' you can declare:
___ e;
explanation: Once the struct is defined, the name is a usable type. Use it like int.
C++ struct definitions REQUIRE a trailing semicolon after };.

spec: Write just the closing two characters of a struct definition (close-brace and semicolon)
explanation: Every struct definition ends with }; (close brace + semicolon).

spec: Complete the struct: employee_data with int id and string name. End with };.
explanation: Standard SIT102 employee_data struct shape.

spec: Write a struct named 'employee_data' with three fields: int id, string name, double salary. End with };.
explanation: This is one of the canonical Q2 struct shapes.

spec: Write a struct named 'employee_data' with these fields: int id, string name, string department. End with };.
explanation: Three fields. Two strings + one int. Trailing };.

spec: Write a struct named 'employee_data' with: int id, double hours, double rate. End with };.
explanation: Mix of int and doubles. Each field is one line ending in ;.

steps.annotation: Field syntax: TYPE space NAME semicolon. Just like declaring a normal variable.
Each field on its own line. NEVER write 'int x, y;' inside a struct (style for SIT102).
Field type 'double', name 'x', terminating ';'. Reads like a single-variable declaration.
Same pattern again for y. The two fields are independent slots.
First field: int x;
Mixed-type fields are normal: int, int, string -- each its own line.

teachMe: Two int fields x and y; assigned then printed with comma between them.
double fields used in arithmetic. 1.5 + 2.5 = 4 (no decimal because exact).
Three fields with different types. Print just the label string field.
Two point instances each with their own x and y. Output: '1 9'.

explanation: Each field ends with a semicolon. 'int x;' is one field declaration.
Field syntax: TYPE NAME ;. Here 'int x;'.
Both fields are int. Type comes before name.
Two separate field declarations: 'int x;' and 'int y;'.

spec: Write a single field declaration: type int, name x
expectedAnswer: int x;
explanation: Field syntax: TYPE NAME ;. So 'int x;'.

spec: Complete: struct point with two int fields named x and y. End with };.
explanation: Each field on its own line: int x; then int y;

spec: Write a struct named 'point' with three fields: int x, int y, string label. End with };.
explanation: Three field declarations, mixed int and string.

spec: Write a struct named 'point' with two double fields: x and y. End with };.
explanation: double fields. Same pattern: TYPE NAME ;.

spec: Write a struct named 'point' with: int x, int y, double weight. End with };.
explanation: Mixed int and double fields, each its own line.

steps.annotation: Close brace immediately followed by semicolon. Forgetting the ; is the #1 SIT102 Q2 error.
Without the };, this line would generate a confusing compiler error.
Struct definition: } SEMICOLON. The struct is a definition statement.
Function body: } only, no semicolon. Different rule from struct.
Begin a struct definition.
End with }; -- this is a complete C++ statement.
Now rectangle works as a type. Output: 'area=10' (2.5 * 4.0).

teachMe: 5.0 * 3.0 = 15.0; cout shows '15' (trailing zero not displayed by default).
Perimeter: 2 * (4 + 6) = 20.
2.5 * 4.0 = 10. Output prefixed with 'area='.

clozeSentence: Final character of a struct definition:
explanation: Struct must end with }; -- the semicolon after } is mandatory.

clozeSentence: Two characters that close the struct definition:
explanation: }; -- the closing brace then a semicolon. Both are required.

clozeSentence: Character that follows } for struct (but not function):
explanation: Struct ends with };  Functions end with } only.
Yes -- C++ requires the trailing semicolon after the closing }.

spec: Write the two characters that close every struct definition (close brace then semicolon)
explanation: }; -- close brace + semicolon. Mandatory.

spec: Complete this struct definition by adding the proper closing (don't forget };)
explanation: }; closes the struct. Common mistake is }, alone -- that's a compile error.

spec: Write a struct named 'rectangle' with two int fields w and h. End with };.
explanation: Two int fields, struct ending with };.

spec: Write a struct named 'rectangle' with double width, double height, string color. End with };.
explanation: Three fields, struct ending };.

spec: Write a struct named 'rectangle' with int w, int h, bool filled. End with };.
explanation: Mixed types in struct fields. Don't forget };.

levelLabel: L11 -> using the struct as a type (weather)

steps.annotation: Use weather_record as a TYPE -- like int or double. 'today' is one weather_record.
today is a single weather_record value. Access its fields with the dot.
Each declaration creates a fresh independent struct value.
tuesday and monday are completely separate.

levelLabel: L11 -> no struct keyword at use-site

steps.code: // C++: just write the type name, NO 'struct' keyword needed
steps.annotation: Unlike C, C++ doesn't need 'struct' before the name when declaring an instance.
Just the type name -- 'weather_record' -- followed by the variable name.

teachMe: weather_record used as a type. today is one instance with two fields.
Two independent weather_records. Each has its own temp field.
Reassigning fields overwrites them. Final humidity = 80.
Field value can be assigned to a normal double. r.temp reads the field.

code:
struct weather_record { double temp; };
___ today;
explanation: Use the struct's name as a type, just like 'int' or 'double'.

code:
struct weather_record { double temp; };
weather_record ___;
explanation: After the type comes the variable name -- here 'today'.

clozeSentence: What kind of name comes first when declaring a struct instance?
explanation: Pattern: TYPE varName; -- like 'int x;' or 'weather_record today;'.

clozeSentence: Language used in SIT102 (C or C++):
explanation: SIT102 is C++. In C++ you don't need 'struct' before the type at use-site.

spec: Given `struct weather_record { double temp; };` -- write the line that declares an instance named 'today'
expectedAnswer: weather_record today;
explanation: Type name + space + variable name + semicolon. Just like int.

spec: Define a struct weather_record with double temp, then declare an instance named 'today' inside main
explanation: Define the type first, then declare an instance using that type.

spec: Write a complete program: struct weather_record with double temp and int humidity. main() declares an instance 'today', sets today.temp = 22.5 and today.humidity = 65, prints them separated by space.
explanation: struct + instance + field writes + cout. Standard SIT102 program shape.

spec: Write a complete program: struct weather_record with double temp. main() declares instance 'reading', sets reading.temp = 30.0, prints reading.temp.
explanation: Single-field struct, declared as a type, used in main.

spec: Write a complete program: struct weather_record with int humidity. Declare TWO instances 'mon' and 'tue', set mon.humidity = 60 and tue.humidity = 80. Print mon.humidity then tue.humidity space-separated.
explanation: Two independent struct instances, each with their own field.

steps.annotation: The dot operator '.' selects which field of the struct to access.
Read each field with the dot. s.name and s.gpa work like normal variables in expressions.
Write to a field using s.fieldname = value.
Read the same field by the same syntax (s.name).
You can write to a field many times. Each write overwrites.

steps.code: // s->id = 1;  // ERROR: -> is for pointers, not structs in SIT102
steps.annotation: DO NOT use -> in SIT102. -> is for pointers (you don't have those in Test 2).
Always use . for direct struct access.

teachMe: Use s.id and s.name to write and read fields. Output: '100 Riley'.
Field can be on both sides of =. s.gpa = s.gpa + 0.25 reads then writes.
s.grade is 85; condition s.grade >= 50 is true; print PASS.
Two student_record instances each with their own .id and .name. Output: 'Sam/Lee'.

code:
student_record s;
s___id = 42;
clozeSentence: Operator that accesses a struct field:
explanation: Use the dot . to access fields of a struct value.
After the dot, write the field name. Here: id.

code: // SIT102 uses ___ to access struct fields (single character).
explanation: Dot operator (single character: .) for direct struct field access.
Read the name field via s.name. cout prints 'Riley'.

spec: Given `student_record s;` -- write the line that sets the id field to 100
explanation: Use the dot to select the field, then assign.

spec: Given `student_record s; s.name = "Riley";` -- write the cout that prints just the name field with endl
expectedAnswer: cout << s.name << endl;
explanation: cout << s.name reads the field. endl ends the line.

spec: Given the struct below, complete main: set id to 100, set name to "Riley", set gpa to 3.85
explanation: Three field assignments using the dot operator.

spec: Write a complete program: struct student_record with int id, string name. main() declares instance s, sets s.id=100, s.name="Riley", prints id and name space-separated.
explanation: struct + instance + dot writes + cout reads via dot.

spec: Write a complete program: struct student_record with int id, double gpa. main() sets s.id=12, s.gpa=3.7, prints id then gpa space-separated.
explanation: Each field accessed with dot. Print both.

steps.annotation: Field write: instance.fieldname = value;
Read-modify-write a field: read on RHS, write on LHS.

levelLabel: L11 -> assignment to one field at a time

steps.annotation: Write to one field. Other fields are untouched.
Reassigning a field overwrites it (just like a normal variable).
cin >> is also a field write. The user's number is stored into office.pages.
Then read the field back out and print it.

teachMe: Two field writes, then a print of both fields space-separated.
Three decrements: 100 -> 99 -> 98 -> 97.
Reassigning fields overwrites previous values. Final: status='busy', jobs=5.

code:
printer p;
p.pages_left ___ 500;
clozeSentence: Operator that writes a value to a field:
explanation: Standard assignment: p.pages_left = 500;
100 - 1 = 99. Read-modify-write pattern with the dot.
Use = to assign a value to a struct field.
cin >> reads input directly into a struct field.

spec: Given `printer p;` -- write the line that sets p.pages_left to 500
expectedAnswer: p.pages_left = 500;
explanation: Field assignment: instance.field = value;

spec: Given `printer p; p.pages_left = 100;` -- write the line that decrements p.pages_left by 1 (read-modify-write)
expectedAnswer: p.pages_left = p.pages_left - 1;
explanation: Read p.pages_left, subtract 1, write back.

spec: Given the struct below, complete main: assign printer's model to "LaserJet" and pages_left to 500
explanation: Two field assignments after the instance is declared.

spec: Write a complete program: struct printer with string model and int pages_left. main() declares 'office', sets office.model="LaserJet", office.pages_left=500, prints them space-separated.
explanation: struct + instance + writes + cout. Mirrors Q3-style struct-write programs.

spec: Write a complete program: struct printer with int jobs. main() declares 'p', sets p.jobs = 0, then writes p.jobs = p.jobs + 3 twice, then prints p.jobs. Output: 6.
explanation: Read-modify-write a field. 0 + 3 + 3 = 6.

levelLabel: L11 -> reading a struct field via cout (ticket)

steps.annotation: Read t.seat with the dot, send to cout. Prints 12.
Same syntax for any field. Prints 49.95.

levelLabel: L11 -> read field anywhere expressions go

steps.annotation: t.price reads the field. Use it in any expression.

steps.code: cout << t.holder << " seat " << t.seat << " $" << t.price << endl;
steps.annotation: Chain << to print multiple fields with literal text in between.

teachMe: Two cout lines, each printing one field of t.
Reading t.price (80.0) used in arithmetic. 80 * 0.9 = 72.
Chained cout reads multiple fields with text between. Output: 'Mira seat 7'.
Field reads in expression: 20 + 30 = 50.

explanation: cout << reads the field via the dot and writes to terminal.
80 / 2 = 40. Reading t.price in arithmetic.
cout << t.holder reads and prints the holder field.

code:
// Wrong: cout << t   (prints whole struct -- not allowed)
// Right: cout << t.___;
clozeSentence: Word for the named member of a struct (with dot):
explanation: Print specific FIELDS via the dot. C++ doesn't auto-print whole structs.

spec: Given `ticket t; t.seat = 12;` -- write a single line cout that prints t.seat followed by endl
expectedAnswer: cout << t.seat << endl;
explanation: cout << t.seat reads the field. Followed by endl to end the line.

spec: Given `ticket t; t.price = 49.95;` -- write a cout line that prints just t.price with endl
expectedAnswer: cout << t.price << endl;
explanation: Read the price field via dot and print it.

spec: Given the struct below, complete main: print t.seat and t.price separated by a single space
explanation: Chain << to print two fields with a literal space between.

spec: Write a complete program: struct ticket with int seat, double price. main() declares t, sets seat=12 and price=49.95, prints them on one line space-separated.
explanation: struct + writes + chained cout reads.

spec: Write a complete program: struct ticket with double price. main() declares t, sets price=80.0, prints t.price * 2 with endl.
explanation: Field reads work in any expression -- here multiplied by 2.

levelLabel: L11 -> struct can hold an array (song)

steps.annotation: Field with array: TYPE NAME[SIZE]; -- here a 3-double array.
Access the array field, then index it with [1]. Prints 4.2.

levelLabel: L11 -> array field declared like normal field plus [N]

steps.annotation: Array field: 5 ints stored together as part of song.
Loop fills the array field. Use s.durations[i] just like a normal array.
Array field of 5 doubles -- exactly like the Q1 stat_double pattern.
Scalar double field beside the array.
Read the array element (s.numbers[0]) and write it into s.mystery.

teachMe: Array field filled at three indices; print element at index 1. Output: 4.2.

Loop fills s.durations[0..3] with 0..3. Print s.durations[3] = 3.

explanation: Array field: TYPE NAME[SIZE]; -- the size goes inside [ ].
int is the element type. Whole field is 'int durations[5];'.
Two fields: an array and a scalar. Both are members.
C++ arrays are 0-indexed. First element is at index 0.

spec: Write a single field declaration: array of 5 doubles named 'numbers'
expectedAnswer: double numbers[5];
explanation: Field with array: TYPE NAME[SIZE];

explanation: One array field inside the struct.

spec: Write a struct named 'song' with two fields: string title, double track_lengths[3]. End with };.
explanation: A struct can hold both scalar and array fields.

spec: Write a struct named 'song' modeled on stat_double: double numbers[5], double mystery. End with };.
explanation: Q1 stat_double-style. Array + scalar.

spec: Write a struct named 'song' with: string title, int durations[4]. End with };.
explanation: Two fields: a string and an array of 4 ints.

steps.annotation: Read left to right: 'c' is the contact, .phones is the array field, [0] is the first element.
Same pattern for reading. c.phones[0] is one string slot.
c.emails: pick the array field. [0]: pick the first element of that field.
Same chain to read the second element of the array field.
Write to chain: c.scores[0]. Same shape as Q1's data.numbers[i].
Read c.scores[0] and copy into c.mystery -- mimics who_am_i Q1 pattern.

teachMe: Three writes to the array field at distinct indices. Read c.phones[1]: '555-0102'.
Q1 pattern: read c.scores[2] = 9.0, copy into c.mystery.
Loop fills c.counts[i] = i*i. c.counts[3] = 9.
Sum chained accesses: c.values[0] + c.values[1] + c.values[2] = 6.

explanation: Arrays are 0-indexed. First slot is [0].
[2] picks the third slot of c.scores. Chain: c -> .scores -> [2].

code:
// Chain order: c ___ scores ___ 0 ___
// (correct: c.scores[0])

explanation: Dot first to reach the field, then [index] to pick an element.
Dot first (to pick the field), then bracket (to index that field's array).

explanation: Chain: c.phones[0]. Then = and the value.

expectedAnswer: cout << c.scores[1] << endl;

explanation: Three chained-access writes to the array field.

explanation: struct + array field + chained access.

steps.annotation: An array of 3 sensors. Each list[i] is a full sensor struct.
Print the id of the second sensor: list[1].id.

levelLabel: L11 -> standard array syntax with struct element type

steps.annotation: Same shape as 'int x[2];' but element type is sensor instead of int.

Each list[i] has its own id and value fields. Two sensors, two output lines.

explanation: Size goes inside [ ]. Here we want 3 elements.
Use the struct name as the array element type, like int or double.

code: // 'sensor list[3]' creates ___ sensor instances (number)

SIT102 convention: 'list' for the array of structs in Q3/Q4.

expectedAnswer: sensor list[3];

explanation: Define struct, then declare an array of that struct.

explanation: struct + array of structs + chained writes/reads (matches Q3/Q4).

explanation: Two struct array elements; sum their value field.

levelLabel: L11 -> bracket then dot per element

steps.annotation: Pattern: list[i] picks element, .amount picks field, = writes value.

question: Type the canonical employee_data struct: int id, string name, double salary
Type the canonical point struct: int x, int y
Type the canonical rectangle struct: double width, double height
Type a complete program: struct student_record (int id, string name), main declares s, sets s.id=12345 and s.name="Riley", prints both

Type the book struct: string title, string author, int year

examples.label: Example B: struct rectangle

prompt: Write a struct named 'point' with one int field named 'value'. Don't forget };
Given `struct weather_record { double temp; };` -- write the line that declares an instance named 'today'.
expectedAnswer: weather_record today;
